subject: Mathematics
q: The statement $p \rightarrow(q \rightarrow p)$ is equivalent to
question: The statement $p \rightarrow(q \rightarrow p)$ is equivalent to
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& p \rightarrow(q \rightarrow p)=\sim p \vee(q \rightarrow p) \\
& =\sim p \vee(\sim q \vee p) \quad \text { since } p \vee \sim p \text { is always true } \\
& =\sim p \vee p \vee q=p \rightarrow(p \vee q) .
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Mathematics
q: Negation of the statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>⇒</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is :
question: Negation of the statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>⇒</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is :
solution:
The negation of the statement (p∨r)⇒(q∨r) is given by

~p∨r⇒q∨r

≡~~p∨r∨q∨r

≡p∨r∧~q∨r

≡p∨r∧~q∧~r

≡p∨r∧~r∧~q

≡p∧~r∨r∧~r∧~q

≡((p∧~r)∨ϕ)∧(~q)

≡(p∧~r)∧(~q)

≡p∧~q∧~r

solution:
~~p∧p∨q

p∨~p∧~q

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $\mathrm{p}, \mathrm{q}, \mathrm{r}$ denote arbitrary statements. Then the logically equivalent of the statement $\mathrm{p} \Rightarrow(\mathrm{q} \vee \mathrm{r})$ is:
question: Let $\mathrm{p}, \mathrm{q}, \mathrm{r}$ denote arbitrary statements. Then the logically equivalent of the statement $\mathrm{p} \Rightarrow(\mathrm{q} \vee \mathrm{r})$ is:
solution:
Given statement is
$p \Rightarrow(q \vee r)$ which is equivalent to
$$
(p \Rightarrow q) \vee(p \Rightarrow r)
$$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Δ</mi><mo>,</mo><mo>∇</mo><mo>∈</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mo>∧</mo><mo>,</mo><mo>∨</mo></mrow></mfenced></math> be such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>∇</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>→</mo><mfenced><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mi>Δ</mi><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∇</mo><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is a tautology. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∇</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∆</mo><mi>r</mi></math> is logically equivalent to
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Δ</mi><mo>,</mo><mo>∇</mo><mo>∈</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mo>∧</mo><mo>,</mo><mo>∨</mo></mrow></mfenced></math> be such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>∇</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>→</mo><mfenced><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mi>Δ</mi><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∇</mo><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is a tautology. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∇</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∆</mo><mi>r</mi></math> is logically equivalent to
solution:
Case-I

If Δ≡∇≡∧

p∧q→p∧q∧r

then it is false if r is false.

So not a tautology

Case-II

If Δ≡∇≡∨

p∨q→p∨q∨r≡ tautology

then p∨q∨r≡pΔr∨q

Case-III

If Δ=∨,∇=∧

then p∧q→p∨q∧r is not a tautology

when p→T,q→T,r→F

Case-IV

If Δ=∧,∇=∨

p∧q→p∧q∨r is not a tautology

subject: Mathematics
q: The Boolean expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>⇒</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>∧</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>⇒</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is equivalent to :
question: The Boolean expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>⇒</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>∧</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>⇒</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is equivalent to :
solution:
(p⇒q)∧(q⇒~p)

=(~p∨q)∧(~q∨~p)  {p⇒q=~p∨q}

=(~p∨q)∧(~p∨~q)  {commutative property}

=~p∨(q∧~q)   {distributive property}

=~p

solution:
Given p∧r⇔p∧~q≡~p

Assuming all option one by one,

Taking r=p

p q ~p ~q p∧p p∧~q p∧r⇔p∧~q
T T F F T F F
T F F T T T T
F T T F F F T
F F T T F F T

Now taking r=~p

p q ~p ~q p∧~p p∧~q p∧r⇔p∧~q
T T F F F F T

F T T F F F T
F F T T F F T

p q ~p ~q p∧q p∧~q p∧r⇔p∧~q

q:

$q$ : I shall play tennis in the afternoon
The negation of the statement "If the sun is shining then I shall play tennis in the afternoon", is
question:
Let $p$ and $q$ denote the following statements $p$ : The sun is shining
$q$ : I shall play tennis in the afternoon
The negation of the statement "If the sun is shining then I shall play tennis in the afternoon", is
solution:
Let $p$ : The sun is shining. $q$ : I shall play tennis in the afternoon.
Negation of $p \rightarrow q$ is $\sim(p \rightarrow q)=p \wedge \sim q$

subject: Mathematics
q: The contrapositive of the statement ''if I am not feeling well, then I will go to the doctor" is
question: The contrapositive of the statement ''if I am not feeling well, then I will go to the doctor" is
options: if I will go to the doctor, then I am not feeling well.
if I am feeling well, then I will not go to the doctor.
if I will not go to the doctor, then I am feeling well.
if I will go to the doctor, then I am feeling well.
solution:
Statement: If I am not feeling well, then I will go to the doctor.

Let p: I am not feeling well and q: I will go to the doctor.

The given statement is p→q.

We know that the contrapositive of p→q is ~q→~p.

Contrapositive: If I will not go to the doctor, then I am feeling well.

solution:
~p∧~q

=~p∨~~q By De Morgan's Laws.

=~p∨q

If the squares of two numbers are not equal, then the numbers are equal

p→q

So, answer is (d) - If the squares of two numbers are equal, then the numbers are equal.

subject: Mathematics
q:
Consider :
Statement - I : p∧~q∧~p∧q is a fallacy.
Statement - II : p→q↔~q→~p is a tautology.
question:
Consider :
Statement - I : p∧~q∧~p∧q is a fallacy.
Statement - II : p→q↔~q→~p is a tautology.
options: Statement - <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> true; Statement -<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is true; Statement -<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is a correct explanation for Statement - <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math>.

Statement - <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is true; Statement - <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is true; Statement - <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is not a correct explanation for Statement - <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math>.
solution:
The truth table for Statement I is

p q ~p ~q p∧~q ~p∧q p∧~q∧~p∧q

TTFF

 TFTF FFTT FTFT FTFF FFTF

FFFF

Hence, it is a fallacy. 

The truth table for statement II is

p q ~p ~q p→q ~q→~p p→q↔~q→~p
TTFF TFTF FFTT FTFT TFTT TFTT TTTT

Hence, it is a tautology.

But, the second statement is not a correct explanation for the first.

question:

S1:~p∨q∨~p∨r and S2:p→q∨r
Then, which of the following is NOT true?
solution:
S1:~p∨q∨~p∨r

≡~p∨q∨r

S2:p→q∨r≡~p∨q∨r

So S1 is equivalent to S2

q: If the Boolean expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>⊕</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∧</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>⊙</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equivalent to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>⊕</mo><mo>,</mo><mo>⊙</mo><mo>∈</mo><mfenced open="{" close="}" separators="|"><mrow><mo>∧</mo><mo>,</mo><mo>∨</mo></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi></math> then the ordered pair <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>⊕</mo><mo>,</mo><mo>⊙</mo></mrow></mfenced></math> is
question: If the Boolean expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>⊕</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∧</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>⊙</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equivalent to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>⊕</mo><mo>,</mo><mo>⊙</mo><mo>∈</mo><mfenced open="{" close="}" separators="|"><mrow><mo>∧</mo><mo>,</mo><mo>∨</mo></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi></math> then the ordered pair <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>⊕</mo><mo>,</mo><mo>⊙</mo></mrow></mfenced></math> is
solution:
1) p∨q∧~p∧q

≡p∨q∧~p∧q (Using associative property)

≡q∧~p∧q

≡~p∧q

2) =p∧q∧~p∧q

≡p∧~p∧q

≡F∧q≡F

3) p∨q∧~p∨q

≡p∧~p∨q

⇒F∨q≡q

4) p∧q∧~p∨q

≡q∧p∧~p∨q

≡q∧p∧~p∨q (using associative property)

≡q∧p∧q

≡p∧q

q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>P</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>,</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> be such that the logical statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi><mo>∨</mo><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>p</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>⇒</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∨</mo><mi>r</mi></math> is a tautology. subject: Mathematics
q: The Statement that is TRUE among the following is
question: The Statement that is TRUE among the following is
options: The contrapositive of $3 x+2=8 \Rightarrow x=2$ is $x \neq 2$ $\Rightarrow 3 x+2 \neq 8$.
The converse of $\tan \mathrm{x}=0 \Rightarrow x=0$ is $x \neq 0 \Rightarrow$ $\tan x=0$.
$p \vee q$ and $p \wedge q$ have the same truth table.
solution:
Only statement given in option
(a) is true.
(b) The converse of $\tan \mathrm{x}=0 \Rightarrow x=0$ is $x=0 \Rightarrow \tan x=0$
$\therefore$ Statement (b) is false
(c) $\sim(p \Rightarrow q$ is dquivalent to $p \wedge \sim q$
$\therefore$ Statement given in option (c) is false.
(d) No, $p \vee q$ and $p \wedge q$ does not have the same truth value.

subject: Mathematics
q: The contrapositive of the following statement, "If the side of a square doubles, then its area increases four times", is
question: The contrapositive of the following statement, "If the side of a square doubles, then its area increases four times", is
options: if the area of a square increases four times, then its side is not doubled.
if the area of a square increases four times, then its side is doubled.
if the area of a square does not increase four times, then its side is not doubled.
if the side of a square is not doubled, then its area does not increase four times.
solution:
Contrapositive of p→q is given by ~q→~p.

So, contrapositive of given statement is, "If the area of a square does not increase four times, then its side is not doubled".

subject: Mathematics
q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> are two statements, then which of the following compound statement is a tautology?
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> are two statements, then which of the following compound statement is a tautology?
solution:
LHS of all the options are same i.e. ((P→Q)∧~Q)

≡(~P∨Q)∧~Q

≡(~P∧~Q)∨(Q∧~Q)

≡~P∧~Q

(A) (~P∧~Q)→Q

≡~(~P∧~Q)∨Q

≡(P∨Q)∨Q≠ tautology

(B) (~P∧~Q)→~P

≡~(~P∧~Q)∨~P

≡(P∨Q)∨~P

(C) (~P∧~Q)→P

≡(P∨Q)∨P≠ Tautology

(D) (~P∧~Q)→(P∧Q)

≡(P∨Q)∨(P∧Q)≠ Tautology

solution:

r: It is Sunday
s: It is Saturday

Then the statement "Ramesh listens to music only if he is in his village and it is Sunday or Saturday" can be expressed as

subject: Mathematics
q:
Consider the following three statements:

A If 3+3=7 then 4+3=8

B If 5+3=8 then earth is flat.

C If both A and B are true then 5+6=17.

Then, which of the following statements is correct?
question:
Consider the following three statements:

A If 3+3=7 then 4+3=8

B If 5+3=8 then earth is flat.

C If both A and B are true then 5+6=17.

Then, which of the following statements is correct?
solution:
Truth table for if-then statement is

p q p→q
T T T
T F F
F T T
F F T

A 3+3=7 (false), and 4+3=8 (false), therefore A is true.

B 5+3=8 (true) and earth is flat (false), hence B is false.

C Since, B is false and 5+6=17 (false), hence C is true.

q: The Boolean expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∨</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∨</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mo>∧</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equivalent to
question: The Boolean expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∨</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∨</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mo>∧</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equivalent to
solution:
p∧q ≡b

p∨~q ≡a+b+d

~p∧~q ≡d

∴p∧q∨p∨~q≡a+b+d

p∧q∨p∨~q∧~p∧~q≡d≡~p∧~q

The negation of the statement, "Suman is brilliant and dishonest if and only if Suman is rich" can be equivalently expressed as

q: The logical statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]" separators="|"><mrow><mo>~</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∨</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mo>∧</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equivalent to
question: The logical statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]" separators="|"><mrow><mo>~</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∨</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mo>∧</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equivalent to
solution:
~~p∨q∨p∧r∧~q∧r

=p∧~q∨p∧r∧~q∧r

By distributive law

=p∧~q∨r∧~q∧r

p∧~q∧r≡p∧r∧~q

=p∧∼q∧r

By Associative law

=p∧r∧~q
